coredata.dc:title: Silicon quantum dots and MOFs hybrid multicolor fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and visual intelligent sensing of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lina
Chen, Junyu
Zhang, Feng
Xu, Jun
Bi, Ning
Gou, Jian
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with multiple emission centers have recently bloomed out as attractive fluorescence (FL) sensors due to their high sensitivity and selectivity to multiple target species. Herein, a novel silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) hybride MOFs multicolor fluorescent nanosensor (SiQDs@MOF-Eu) was elaborately tailored for tetracycline detection, using in situ composition of SiQDs@MOF and further decoration of Eu3+ on its surface due to the coordination of free carboxyl groups and Eu3+. In this nanosensor, both the SiQDs with blue emission (λem=472 nm) and Eu3+ ions with potential red FL (λem=616 nm) can be served as specific recognition units of TC based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and the antenna effect, respectively. Upon the addition of TC, the emission intensity of the SiQDs decreased slowly while that of the Eu3+ ion increased significantly, accompanied by obvious blue-red color switching, resulting in ultrahigh sensitivity (detection limit 3.16 nM). Furthermore, real-time visual determination of TC in actual samples was successfully achieved. With the assistance of color scanning APP in smart phones, real-time intelligent determination of TC can be realized.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Multicolor fluorescence
Real-time visual detection
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of TC using the SiQDs@MOF-Eu nanosensor
Determination of TC in actual samples
Synthesis of TC visual test paper
Detection of TC by ratio FL method
Selectivity of the nanosensor for TC detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071 , No. U1904168 , and No. 51773052 ), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015 ), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (No. 222300420047 ), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No. 222102320446 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province ( NSFRF210331 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and size distribution images of SiQDs (a), TEM images of UiO-66-(COOH)2 (b) and SiQDs@MOF-Eu (c), SEM images of UiO-66-(COOH)2 (d) and SiQDs@MOF-Eu (e), EDS spectrum of SiQDs@MOF-Eu (f).
PXRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of SiQDs, UiO-66-(COOH)2 and SiQDs@MOF-Eu.
XPS spectra of SiQDs and SiQDs@MOF-Eu (a), high-resolution XPS spectra of Si2p (b), O1s (c), C1s (d), N1s (e) and Eu3d (f).
pH optimization of SiQDs@MOF-Eu system in response to TC.

(a) FL spectra of the nanosensor in the presence of various concentrations of TC, (b) the FL discoloration images of SiQDs@MOF-Eu in the presence of various concentrations of TC under UV lamp (λ = 365 nm), (c) linear relationship between the ratio of emission intensity (I616/I472) and TC concentration, (d) CIE coordinates in the presence of various concentrations of TC.

The response of SiQDs@MOF-Eu to TC and interfering species (a) and photos of the corresponding cuvettes under the UV light (b), the response of SiQDs@MOF-Eu to TC in the presence of interfering species (c) and photos of the corresponding cuvettes under the UV light (d), the response of SiQDs@MOF-Eu to different common antibiotics (e) and photos of the corresponding cuvettes under the UV light (f) (the concentration of TC and influencing species were all 1 μM).

(a) UV–visible spectrum of TC, FL excitation and emission spectra of SiQDs, (b) FL lifetime of SiQDs at different doses of TC, (c) UV–visible spectra of TC, SiQDs@MOF-Eu and SiQDs@MOF-Eu with TC, (d) FL lifetime of Eu3+ with and without the addition of TC.

(a) The nanosensor was loaded on the test paper and irradiated in various conditions, (b) Titrations of different concentrations of TC (0, 0.4, 3.3, 3.8, 4.2, 5.1, 6.9, 7.3, 8.7 μM) on SiQDs@MOF-Eu excited by 365 nm UV light and intelligent application in TC detection (R/B value obtained by color recognition software).

head.ce:title: Silicon quantum dots and MOFs hybrid multicolor fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and visual intelligent sensing of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan 454000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with multiple emission centers have recently bloomed out as attractive fluorescence (FL) sensors due to their high sensitivity and selectivity to multiple target species. Herein, a novel silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) hybride MOFs multicolor fluorescent nanosensor (SiQDs@MOF-Eu) was elaborately tailored for tetracycline detection, using in situ composition of SiQDs@MOF and further decoration of Eu3+ on its surface due to the coordination of free carboxyl groups and Eu3+. In this nanosensor, both the SiQDs with blue emission (λem=472 nm) and Eu3+ ions with potential red FL (λem=616 nm) can be served as specific recognition units of TC based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and the antenna effect, respectively. Upon the addition of TC, the emission intensity of the SiQDs decreased slowly while that of the Eu3+ ion increased significantly, accompanied by obvious blue-red color switching, resulting in ultrahigh sensitivity (detection limit 3.16 nM). Furthermore, real-time visual determination of TC in actual samples was successfully achieved. With the assistance of color scanning APP in smart phones, real-time intelligent determination of TC can be realized.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline antibiotics are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics mainly produced by Streptomyces, among which tetracycline (TC) is widely used in medical treatment, aquaculture and agriculture [1–4]. With the development of economy, the abuse of TC is becoming more and more serious. The residual pollution caused by the abuse of TC is not only a pollution to the water environment, but also a threat to the entire ecological environment and human food safety [5,6], and also leads to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [7,8], which makes the public health security increasingly serious [9,10]. Strict requirements and specific standards for the use and residues of TC have been globally established to maintain environmental safety and human health [11–13]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop efficient method for ultrasensitive and visual detection of TC to ensure food and environmental safety.

So far, various detection methods have been developed for TC detection, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [14], high performance liquid chromatography [15], and capillary electrophoresis [16], which usually cannot meet the need for on-site rapid detection because of their complex pretreatments, expensive apparatus, tedious determination and long time-consuming. To date, multiple fluorescence (FL) detection methods [17–19] have attracted much attention due to their merits including facile operation, high specificity and easy realization of naked eye detection [20–22]. Various fluorescent materials involving organic dye molecules, semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, and silicon quantum dots have been exploited for FL detection.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a fascinating class of porous crystal materials which promise large potential in various fields [23,24]. In particular, luminescent MOFs have become potential chemical sensors in recent years because of their easily induced luminescence and structural diversity. Among them, lanthanide MOFs (Ln-MOFs) are considered particularly important because of their intense, long-lived, sharp emission in the visible region [25]. However, it is still a challenge to reasonably design and prepare desired Ln-based MOFs due to the high coordination number and variable nature of Ln3+ coordination sphere. In recent years, growing number of Ln-doped MOFs have been prepared by postsynthetic modifification (PSM). The luminescence of lanthanides can be regulated by introducing small organic molecules (tetracycline or DPA) into the organic networks [26]. However, since the emission intensity is susceptible to a lot of external factors, the fluorescent sensor holds single signal often encounters significant limitations. The ratiometric FL sensor containing self-calibration ability can minimize the interference of external factors, to obtain more accurate and sensitive results [3,27]. In addition, the ratiometric FL sensor with strong color changes are more sensitive, which is conducive to visual on site detection [28–30]. Therefore, extensive exploration of MOFs-based ratiometric sensors with dual/multiple emission centers are of great significance. Fortunately, the ratiometric FL sensor can be realized by embedding other luminescent species into the pore of Ln-MOFs. Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) are good candidates for encapsulation, which exhibit great application prospect in FL sensing because of their prominent merits including nontoxicity [31,32], excellent photostability, outstanding biocompatibility [33] and surface modifiability [34].

Herein, we present a strategy to construct MOF hybrid systems with dual emission centers for ultrasensitive and visual detection of TC. Two main methods, in situ composition and coordinated postsynthetic modifification (PSM), were employed to functionalize the MOF host UiO-66-(COOH)2 with SiQDs and Eu3+ respectively, to obtain ratiometric FL sensor SiQDs@MOF-Eu. By introducing TC, the FL signal of SiQDs decreased slowly because of the inner filter effect, while the emission of Eu3+ enhanced greatly due to the antenna effect. Meanwhile, this nanosensor exhibits distinct blue-red color switching, realizing visual and high sensitive detection of TC. With the assistance of color scanning APP in smart phones, real-time intelligent determination of TC can be realized. ( Fig. 1).

(3-aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS), 1,2,4,5-phenyltetracarboxylic acid (H4BTCC), ascorbate sodium (AS), tetracycline (TC), europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3.6 H2O), trihydroxymethylaminomethane (Tris), and zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) are obtained from China Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. They were all analytical grade and used without purification.

Tecnai G2 F30 microscope was used to record transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and measure energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The accelerating voltage was 300 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured by KBr disk technology within 4000–400 cm−1, and this model was Brooke V70. The images of scanning electron microscope (SEM) were recorded, and Merlin Compact microscope with 5 kV acceleration voltage was used here. The rotating target x-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the sample powder (2θ = 5°−60°) was collected. The composition of the materials surface were determined by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi). The FL spectra were collected by F97 Pro FL spectrometer under the excitation of 380 nm. FL lifetimes were studied using a cooled R928P photomultiplier tube equipped with Edinburgh FS5. UV–visible absorption spectrum was monitored on Lambda950.

A modified facile method was used to prepare the SiQDs [35]. In short, 10 mL of APTMS was added into 40 mL of distilled water heated at 60 °C. The mixture was stirred for 30 min, and then 13.5 mL AS (100 mM) was added into the mixture and stirred for 4 h in oil bath at 80 °C. Then the mixture was centrifuged to remove the precipitation at room temperature. A cellulose membrane (MWCO 500) was used to purify the obtained supernatant in ultrapure water for 48 h. Finally, the powder of SiQDs was obtained by freeze drying.

The method of in situ composition was used to functionalize the MOF host UiO-66-(COOH)2 with SiQDs. 0.0303 g ZrCl4 (0.13 mmol), 0.03274 g H4BTCC (0.13 mmol) and 0.035 g SiQDs were dispersed in 20 mL deionized water and stirred at 25 °C for 3 h. After heating at 120 °C for 24 h, the final product was washed by ethanol and dried for further use.

The method of coordinated postsynthetic modifification (PSM) was applied to functionalize the SiQDs@MOF composite with Eu3+ due to the coordination of Eu3+ and free carboxyl groups on the surface of SiQDs@MOF, achieving ratiometric FL sensor SiQDs@MOF-Eu. In detail, 50 mg SiQDs@MOF was added in 20 mL ethanol, in which the pH was regulated to 7.5 using NaOH solution. After that, Eu(NO3)3.6 H2O (8.5 mL, 0.02 M) was added into the above solution and stirred in an oil bath at 60 °C for 8 h. Then the mixture was washed with pure water and ethanol. After drying, the powder of SiQDs@MOF-Eu nanosensor was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of TC using the SiQDs@MOF-Eu nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: 200 μL of the nanosensor solution (2 mg/mL) was dropped into the mixed solution of sodium alginate and Tris-HCl buffer (pH=9.0) to obtain 2 mL solution. Then various concentration of TC (0–10 μM) was added. After incubation for 14 s, the FL test was carried out at the optimal excitation wavelength (380 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of TC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of pure milk referred to previous reports [36,37]: trichloroacetic acid (300 g/L) was dropped to milk with a volume ratio of 1:100. After ultrasound, the supernatant was centrifuged and filtered (0.22 µm), and the pH value was subsequently regulated to 9.0. Honey was diluted 50 times by using buffer solution and filtered. The lake water from the school was filtered and used directly. The supernatant was prepared to various TC standard solutions. After adding the nanosensor, the FL intensity ratio was determined. The actual samples were detected three times. The concentration, recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) of TC were calculated according to the FL spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TC visual test paper
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the common test paper with fixed shapes were immersed in the nanosensor solution for 1 h, so that the composite nanosensor can be fully loaded on the test paper. Then, the test paper was taken out and placed on the slide to make it naturally dry. The test paper after drying obtained the ability to detect TC with high sensitivity. The color changes with specific TC under 365 nm ultraviolet light were observed by naked eyes. The linear relation between the tetracycline concentration and color RGB value can be obtained by the color acquisition and processing program of smartphones.
Two main methods, in situ composition and coordinated postsynthetic modification (PSM), were employed to functionalize the MOF host UiO-66-(COOH)2 with SiQDs.

and Eu3+ respectively, to obtain the ratiometric FL sensor SiQDs@MOF-Eu. The morphology of SiQDs, UiO-66-(COOH)2 and SiQDs@MOF-Eu were characterized by TEM and SEM. Fig. 2a illustrated that SiQDs displayed good monodispersity with the average diameter of about 5.5 nm. As portrayed in Fig. 2b and c, the as-synthesized nanosensor exhibited the same morphology with the precursor UiO-66-(COOH)2, in which multiple SiQDs can be obviously observed in the pores of the networks (Fig. 2c). As delineated in Fig. 2d and e, the SEM image of the as-synthesized nanosensor displayed monodisperse nanoparticles with cubic shape, which was similar to that of the precursor UiO-66-(COOH)2, indicating the intact frameworks of the obtained nanosensor. In addition, EDS spectrum in Fig. 2f indicated Eu peaks can also be observed besides that of Si, Zr, C and O, manifesting the successful coordination of Eu3+ in the nanosensor.

The structure of the nanosensor was also characterized by PXRD. As portrayed in Fig. 3a, all the characteristic diffraction peaks of the synthesized SiQDs@MOF-Eu composite were in good agreement with that of synthesized and simulated UiO-66-(COOH)2, indicating that the skeleton of the composites was not destroyed during the postsynthetic process.

The chemical composition of SiQDs, UiO-66-(COOH)2 and SiQDs@MOF-Eu composite were further analyzed by FT-IR data (Fig. 3b). Due to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of C-H bond on silane, the absorption peak appeared at 2921 cm−1 [1,2], while the peak located at 1067 cm−1 was related to the Si-O bond. The stretching vibration of N-H bond occurred at 1608 cm−1 and 3435 cm−1 [35]. The observed vibration band at 657 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 indicated that the carboxylic acid group had the tensile vibration of C-O and that of Zr-O, which confirmed the Zr-coordinated MOF structure of UiO-66-(COOH)2 [38]. At the same time, the 1399 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1 peaks were related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxyl group of UiO-66-(COOH)2 [39]. For the successfully prepared UiO-66-(COOH)2, an additional absorption peak was observed at 1713 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching of UiO-66-(COOH)2 at CO in deionized water [40]. In the spectrum of SiQDs@MOF-Eu, as SiQDs replaced the coordination water molecules and entered the pores of the skeleton, the peak near 1713 cm−1 disappeared, and the Si-O bond blue shifted from 1067 cm−1 to 1039 cm−1, indicating that SiQDs were fixed in the skeleton. The characteristic bands of SiQDs@MOF-Eu near 1571 cm−1 and 1389 cm−1 correspond to the νas (-COO-) and νs (-COO-) stretching vibrations of BTCC carboxylic acid groups [41]. Due to the successful coordination between the carboxyl group and Eu3+, the peak of the carboxyl group of the composite shifted relative to the carboxyl group of UiO-66-(COOH)2 [28]. It is not difficult to see that the SiQDs@MOF-Eu nanosensor was successfully synthesized.

The chemical bond and elemental compositions of SiQDs and the nanosensor were further determined by XPS. Peaks located at 102.7, 285.7, 401.5 and 530.8 eV were correspond to Si2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively ( Fig. 4a), while that of the nanosensor showed additional peaks at 183.3, 1135.6 and 1165.3 eV, which were caused by Zr 3d5, Eu 3d5 and Eu 3d3, respectively, manifesting the formation of SiQDs@MOF-Eu. As shown in Fig. 4b–f, the high-resolution XPS images of Si2p, O1s, C1s, N1s further manifested the successful synthesis of the nanosensor. Specifically, for Si2p (Fig. 4b), the peaks at 102.5, 101.9 and 101.2 eV were related to Si-O, Si-N and Si-C bonds, respectively [44]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c), the peaks at 530.5, 531.37 and 532.07 eV indicated the existence of Si-O, CO and C-O bonds respectively [44]. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 4d), the four peaks at 284.1, 284.8, 285.7 and 288.1 eV can be attributed to the C-Si, C-C, C-N and CO bonds, respectively [42]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 4e), the peaks at 399.5 eV, 401.1 eV and 406.6 eV were assigned to the C-N, N-H and N-O bonds, respectively [43]. The multiple interactions of spin orbits lead to the splitting of the Eu3d energy spectrum into four strong peaks (Fig. 4f). The peaks at 1124.57, 1134.82, 1154.55 and 1164.43 eV were affiliated to Eu2+ 3d5/2, Eu3+ 3d5/2, Eu2+ 3d3/2 and Eu3+ 3d3/2 [28,45,46], respectively. Notably, the energy difference between the two peaks of Eu3+ 3d was 29.61 eV, which was very consistent with the coordination energy difference between Eu3+ and O atom previously reported [47,48], which verified the synthesis of the nanosensor.

In addition, the FL spectra of SiQDs and the nanosensor were also investigated. The emission peaks of SiQDs slightly varied under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. S1a), which might be caused by the effect of particle size and various distribution of the surface trap states [49,50]. The optimum excitation and emission wavelength located at 400 and 455 nm, respectively (Fig. S1b). As for SiQDs@MOF-Eu, its maximum emission peak slightly red shift to 472 nm, which was still a blue FL emission. For single UiO-66-(COOH)2, there was no peak at 472 nm (Fig. S2), manifesting that the blue FL of the nanosensor originated from SiQDs rather than UiO-66-(COOH)2 nanocrystals. Therefore, the FL properties of SiQDs in UiO-66-(COOH)2 were retained.

Moreover, to investigate the effect of excitation wavelength on the sensor response, the FL spectra of the nanosensor at different excitation wavelength were measured in the presence of 20 μM of TC. As depicted in Fig. S2a, the emission peaks of the nanosensor showed negligible shift under different excitation wavelength. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the FL intensity of Eu3+ gradually increased, and the emission peak of SiQDs gradually became incomplete. In order to ensure the sensitivity of the detection effect and the integrity of the emission peak of SiQDs, 380 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength during the detection of TC.

In order to obtain the best detection effect of the nanosensor for TC, various tests were carried out to optimize the test conditions. Firstly, the pH of TC detection was screened within the pH range of 3–12. As demonstrated in Fig. 5, the highest FL intensity ratio (I616/I472) appeared at pH 9.0, probably because of europium hydroxide precipitation will occur when the pH is too high [44,49,50]. Meanwhile, Fig. S3 indicated the FL intensity ratio (I616/I472) reached the maximum at about 14 s after adding TC, and remained stable within 10 min, which fully indicated that SiQDs@MOF-Eu coordinated with TC very quickly and formed a more stable complex. Moreover, the long-term stability of the as-prepared nanosensor was also tested. As depicted in Fig. S4, no obvious FL intensity variation (< 2 %) of SiQDs@MOF-Eu was observed after 7 days of storage, while nearly 90 % of the FL intensity was maintained after storage for 30 days. The above results manifested the outstanding stability of the nanosensor, which can meet the requirements of TC rapid detection. Therefore, in the following experiments, pH 9.0 and incubation time of 14 s were adopted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC by ratio FL method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum experiment conditions, the detection performance of the nanosensor for TC was systematically evaluated. As delineated in Fig. 6a, only the emission at 472 nm was observed in the absence of TC, which originated from the emission of SiQDs embedded in the MOF networks. In the presence of different amounts (0–10 μM) of TC, fluorescence intensities of the SiQDs decreased slowly, while the fluorescence intensities of Eu3+ at 592, 616, 653 and 695 nm enhanced significantly, which can be attributed to the antenna effect [35,51]. Therefore, the nanosensor with two recognition units for TC can achieve dual and reverse response signals, achieving high detection sensitivity. In addition, the detection process accompanied by distinct blue-red color changes (Fig. 6b), which provided an opportunity for the visualization of TC detection. The corresponding CIE image (Fig. 6d) and coordinates (Table. S1) were also provided.

As illustrated in Fig. 6c, I616/I472 ratios showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of TC in the range of 0–4 μM (I616/I472 =1.51079TC+0.26635), and R2 = 0.99417 [49,50]. The limit of detection (LOD) of the nanosensor was calculated to be 3.5 nM according to the published method [35], which was far lower than the allowable concentration (676 nM) stipulated by the Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Union (225 nM) [47,48].

Moreover, the PXRD of the nanosensor after the sensing test was carried out to confirm its stability. As delineated in Fig. S5, the characteristic diffraction peaks of the SiQDs@MOF-Eu after the sensing test were in good agreement with that of the original SiQDs@MOF-Eu, indicating the prominent stability of the nanosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the nanosensor for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the complexity of the real detection environment, the common influencing ions were screened to confirm the specific selection of the nanosensor. Common metal ions (Mg2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Fe3+ and Ni2+), representative amino acids and antibiotics were tested, which were most likely to react with the nanosensor or tetracycline, leading to changes in FL intensity. As depicted in Fig. 7a, except for TC, the nanosensor had no obvious FL response to the interfering species, indicating the specific selection of TC by the as-prepared nanosensor. As delineated in Fig. 7c, the coexistence of these interfering species led to negligible influence for TC sensing by the nanosensor, exhibiting the anti-interference ability of the nanosensor towards TC detection. As can be seen from Fig. 7e, no obvious signal changes can be noticed in the presence of common antibiotics except TC. The above results manifested that the nanosensor has a significant specific selectivity for TC detection. Moreover, compared with those interfering substances, the visible FL color change caused by TC makes this sensing system easily recognized by the naked eye under UV light (Fig. 7b, d, f), which provided a visual research method for qualitative identification of TC. Therefore, SiQDs@MOF-Eu exhibited a significant specific selectivity for TC detection.

The mechanism of TC detection was discussed and confirmed. In order to explore the mechanism of TC-induced SiQDs quenching, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of TC, excitation spectrum and FL lifetime of SiQDs were analyzed. As demonstrated in Fig. 8a, the absorption peak of TC (357 nm) overlapped partly with the excitation peak of SiQDs. In addition, the FL lifetimes of SiQDs in the nanosensor showed negligible changes (Fig. 8b), which confirmed the quenching mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) [52].

To further investigate the sensing process, absorption spectra of TC, SiQDs@MOF-Eu and SiQDs@MOF-Eu-TC were analyzed [53]. As portrayed in Fig. 8c, compared with the absorption spectrum of SiQDs@MOF-Eu, SiQDs@MOF-Eu-TC displayed a red shift to 362 nm, indicating the formation of TC-Eu3+ complex. Furthermore, the FL lifetime of Eu3+ in the nanosensor was recorded at the excitation of 380 nm, which increased from 43.16 μs to 112.3 μs after adding 7.5 μM TC (Fig. 8d). It may be due to the effective substitution of H2O by TC at the unsaturated site of Eu3+. The removal of H2O obviously eliminated the FL quenching and sensitized Eu3+ through antenna effect [35,51].

To further verify the feasibility of the nanosensor for the detection of TC in actual samples, the standard addition method was adopted. As demonstrated in Table. S2, the recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) in the process of TC detection were in the range of 72.28–119.40 % and 0.288–3.128 %, respectively, manifesting that the nanosensor hold high stability, accuracy and reliability for TC assay in actual samples.

Nowadays, portable and rapid sensing of TC is becoming more and more popular. In this research, a portable, visual and sensitive sensor was designed to detect TC more conveniently by fixing the SiQDs@MOF-Eu sensor on the filter paper. As portrayed in Fig. 9a, the blue FL was displayed at 365 nm UV lamp, which proved the successful immobilization of SiQDs@MOF-Eu nanosensor on the filter paper. With the increasing of TC concentration, distinct color changes (blue-red) of the test paper were easily captured by the naked eye.

Moreover, by combining with smart phones, using its color capture and processing program, FL color can be digitized [41,54]. After scanning by the mobile phone, the RGB value of the FL image can be automatically displayed, so as to determine the TC concentration in the detection solution by calculating the ratio of red (R) to blue (B). As illustrated in Fig. 9b, TC concentrations (x) and R/B value (y) showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–8.7 μM (y = 0.09402x+0.4799, R2 =0.99069), which can be clearly seen that the lowest detectable concentration for visualization of TC was about 79 nM. In summary, the nanosensor designed here successfully realized the simple and sensitive visual detection of TC [55].

Herein, a strategy to construct novel MOF hybrid systems with dual emission centers was proposed for ultrasensitive and visual detection of TC. Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) hybride MOFs multicolor fluorescent nanosensor SiQDs@MOF-Eu was elaborately tailored by in situ composition and coordinated postsynthetic modifification (PSM). Reverse response signals accompanied by obvious color switching can be observed during the detection process, resulting in ultrahigh sensitivity (detection limit 3.16 nM). Real-time intelligent determination of TC was realized with the assistance of color scanning APP in smart phones. This work expanded the application of MOFs-based hybride FL nanosensor in food and water safety supervision.

Lina Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Resources, Review and editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Junyu Chen: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Feng Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Review and editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ning Bi: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jian Gou: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Review and editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071, No. U1904168, and No. 51773052), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (No.222300420047), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No.222102320446), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (NSFRF210331).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129853.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Sci. 592 (2022) 153170, https://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.153170.
